Member-only story

Software Development

DevOps

Ci Cd Pipeline

Web Development

Technical Writing

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Two years ago, a developer on a five-person team merged a feature branch into main on a Friday afternoon. Nobody runs the test suite locally because it takes too long. Someone manually logged into the production server, pulled the latest code, restarted the process by hand, and hoped for the best. Soon after, a customer reported that the checkout page was broken. The bug was caught by an existing test, but nobody ran it before deploying.
This is the exact problem CI/CD was built to fix. It is not about shipping code faster. It is about removing the gap between writing code and knowing that it is safe.
What CI actually catches
Continuous Integration means that every time someone pushes code, a machine automatically builds it and runs the full test suite. It is not just about integrating code often. A pipeline runs on every push without exception, so no one can skip it.
This catches a specific class of bug: the one that works on your machine and breaks on someone else’s. Two developers touch the same function in different branches. Each one’s local tests pass. Merged together, something breaks. CI finds these issues before the code reaches the main branch instead of after.
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
test:
steps:
- run: npm install…
